title: Scotch broth
subtitle: Barley, mutton, and root vegetables simmered low and slow
excerpt: A thick, nourishing Scottish soup built on neck of mutton, pearl barley, and whatever root vegetables are to hand. The barley thickens the liquid as it cooks, and the meat falls from the bone into the broth. This is cold-weather cooking at its most straightforward.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: Scots barley broth, recipe 1160. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Scotch mutton broth with vegetables.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to three months. The barley absorbs liquid on standing; add a splash of water when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily in a large stockpot. The broth improves overnight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Even better the next day. Skim any solidified fat from the surface before reheating.

content.text: Scotch broth is not a delicate soup. It is thick, substantial, and deeply savoury — somewhere between a soup and a stew depending on how much barley you use. Mutton neck is the traditional cut: it has enough fat to enrich the broth and enough connective tissue to soften into yielding strands over two and a half hours. Lamb neck works nearly as well if mutton is hard to find.

Pearl barley is not optional. It thickens the broth and gives the soup its characteristic body. Add it for the final hour of cooking so it swells to a pleasing firmness without turning to paste. The vegetable list is flexible within reason: turnip, swede, carrot, and leek are the standard quartet, but parsnip and celery are welcome additions.

Put the mutton in a large pot and cover with 2.5 litres of cold water. Bring slowly to the boil, skimming off the grey scum that rises to the surface. Once the broth is clear, add the {{onion}} onion, a generous pinch of salt, and a few cracks of pepper. Reduce the heat to a steady simmer. Cook, covered, for 90 minutes.

Lift the mutton out and set aside to cool. Add the {{pearl-barley}} of barley, {{carrot}} carrots, {{swede}} swede, {{turnip}} turnips, and {{leek}} leeks to the pot. Simmer for 45 minutes. While the vegetables cook, pick the mutton meat from the bones, discarding the bone and any large pieces of fat. Return the meat to the pot. Cook for a further 15 minutes.
Taste and adjust the salt. The broth should be thick and substantial, not watery. Stir through the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley and serve in deep bowls with bread on the side.

items.cause: Scum not fully skimmed at the start
items.fix: Bring the water and meat to the boil more slowly next time, and skim vigilantly for the first 10 minutes; a cloudy broth is not harmful but looks unappetising

items.cause: Added too early or cooked too long
items.fix: Add the barley for the final hour only; 45 minutes is sufficient for pearl barley to cook through without disintegrating

items.cause: Insufficient meat or too much water
items.fix: Use at least 800 g of bone-in mutton per 6 servings; a bare simmer extracts more flavour than a rolling boil, which evaporates the liquid without concentrating the meat
Mutton neck needs at least 90 minutes at a steady simmer; the meat should fall from the bone without pulling

content.text: Scotch broth has been made in Scotland for centuries, appearing in household accounts and cookery books from the eighteenth century onward. Mrs Beeton included it as a standard British soup in 1861, and Eliza Acton noted the barley as the defining element that distinguished it from English mutton broth. The dish is frugal by design: a cheap cut of meat, a handful of root vegetables, and a cold afternoon. It is eaten from October to March and reheated the next day, when it is always better.
